nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving streams and tributaries of the upper and middle Orinoco River basin; typically found in soft, dark, tannin-stained waters with abundant vegetation and leaf litter.

decorPreferences: open swimming space in the center

diet.notes: Generally accepts a wide variety of foods including quality dried flakes and nano pellets; tends to benefit from regular offerings of live or frozen foods such as bloodworm, Daphnia, Moina, mosquito larvae, and brine shrimp to maintain color and condition.

summary: A hardy, iridescent red schooling tetra from soft-acidic Orinoco tributaries in Colombia and Venezuela. Peaceful and active; best kept in groups of 6 or more. Generally well-suited to community tanks with similarly sized peaceful fish.
careNotes: Red Phantom Tetras tend to display their best coloration in well-planted tanks with soft, slightly acidic water and subdued lighting — conditions approximating their blackwater Orinoco habitat. Dark substrate and the addition of dried leaf litter or driftwood are commonly reported to help bring out their reds. Males develop elongated dorsal and anal fins and engage in ritualized fin-spreading displays toward one another that are generally non-violent; a group of 6 or more is typically sufficient to distribute this behavior. These fish have been noted to predate on dwarf shrimp and small fry, so care should be taken with nano invertebrates. They are not known to nip plants, unlike the superficially similar serpae tetra (Hyphessobrycon eques). Multiple color forms exist, including a particularly vivid 'red' trade variety. The species was originally described in the genus Megalamphodus before being synonymized with Hyphessobrycon; older references may use the name Megalamphodus sweglesi.
breedingNotes: Has been reported to breed readily in captivity under suitable conditions. Egg-scatterers with no parental care; adults will consume eggs and fry and should be removed after spawning. A dedicated breeding tank of around 57–75 L is generally recommended, with soft, slightly acidic water (pH 5.5–6.5, gH 1–5), dim lighting, and fine-leaved plants or spawning mops to receive the scattered eggs. Eggs tend to hatch within 24–36 hours; fry are initially very small and typically require infusoria or commercial fry food before progressing to micro-worm and baby brine shrimp. Breeding is broadly considered moderate difficulty due to the need for controlled water conditions and fry feeding.

primary.name: Aquarium Co-Op
primary.notes: Primary care guide covering both red and black phantom tetras; used for size, parameters, diet, temperament, tankmates, and grouping data.

additional.name: SeriouslyFish
additional.notes: Cross-checked scientific name, taxonomy, native range, adult size (30–35 mm SL), temperature (20–28°C), pH (4.5–7.5), hardness (18–215 ppm / ~1–12 dGH), minimum tank footprint (80×30 cm), grouping, diet, and breeding notes.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (3.2 cm SL), temperature (20–23°C), pH (5.5–7.5), native range (Orinoco basin), taxonomy confirmation. Temperature upper bound conservative vs. other sources; averaged across all sources.

additional.name: Fish Laboratory
additional.notes: Cross-checked native range (Colombia/Venezuela), temperature (18–26°C), pH (5.5–7.5), gH (4–20 dH), lifespan (5–7 years), grouping (8–12 fish), breeding notes. Reported max size of 5 cm likely includes extended fin length (TL) and was treated as an outlier vs. the 3.0–3.5 cm SL consensus from SeriouslyFish and FishBase.

fish.breedingNotes: Egg-scatterers; controlled breeding tank required with soft acidic water and spawning plants or mops. Adults consume eggs and must be removed. Fry require micro-foods initially.
